coredata.dc:title: Development of nanocomposite membranes based on sulfated β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde with magnetically recoverable magnetite-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles for water vapor dehumidification
coredata.dc:creator: Shirke, Yogita M.
Abou-Elanwar, Ali M.
Kwon, Soon Jin
Choi, Won-Kil
Hong, Seong Uk
Lee, Hyung Keun
Jeon, Jae-Deok
coredata.dc:description: Here, we report sulfated β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde-coated (sb-CD/GA) TFN membranes with highly magnetically recoverable magnetite-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles (CDs-Fe3O4) realized by enhancing the water vapor dehumidification. The Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs are successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method and incorporated into sulfated-β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde (sb-CD/GA)-based thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes. Sb-CD is also very hydrophilic due to the presence of several reactive sites, specifically sulfonic acid groups, on its external surface. Therefore, the addition of hydrophilic sb-CD to the composite membranes facilitates the transport of water vapor. Moreover, one characteristic of Fe3O4–CDs particles is that they have a variety of hydrophilic groups which allow them to absorb more water vapor at a relatively rapid rate, with the addition of nanoparticles in the TFN membranes increasing the water vapor permeation due to the enhanced sorption sites and surface area provided by the nanoparticles. According to experimental results, the addition of Fe3O4–CDs to the sb-CD/GA -coated TFN membrane improves the water vapor permeance from 1376 M(0) to 2232 GPU M(0.3) wt% Fe3O4–CDs loading, improving the water vapor/N2 selectivity as well from 105 M(0) to 724 (M(0.3) at a relative humidity rate of 70%. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of a sb-CD/GA-coated TFN membrane incorporated with highly functionalized Fe3O4–CDs to enhance water vapor dehumidification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Water vapor dehumidification
Thin film nanocomposite membrane
Magnetically recoverable
Magnetite-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles

Physical structure of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs
Chemical structure of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs
Characteristics of the sb-CD/GA TFN membranes
Performance of sb-CD/GA nanocomposite membranes for dehumidification

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was funded by the Climate Change Impact Minimization Technology Development program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Korean government (Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT)) (No. 2020M3H5A1081107 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs.
Characteristics of Fe3O4–CD hybrid nanoparticles according to a-b) HR-TEM, c-h) HR-TEM mapping.
a) Survey scan of the XPS spectrum of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs, and deconvolution spectra of b) C1s, c) O1s, d) N1s, and e) Fe2p.
FT-IR spectrum of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs.
Magnetic hysteresis loops of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs.
FTIR spectra of the a) PSf, b) M(0), c) M(0.1), d) M(0.3), and e) M(0.5) TFN membranes.
SEM surface images of the a) PSf, b) M(0), c) M(0.3), and d) M(0.5) TFN membranes.
AFM three-dimensional surface images of the a) PSf, b) M(0), c) M(0.05), d) M(0.1), e) M(0.3), and f) M(0.5) TFN membranes.
Water contact angle results of the: a) PSf, b) M(0), c) M(0.05), d) M(0.1), and e) M(0.3), and f) M(0.5) TFN membranes.
Effect of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NP loading level on the performance of sb-CD/GA composite membranes at a temperature of 30 °C.
Effect of the RH% on the performance of the M(0.3) membrane at 30 °C.
Water Vapor Separation Capabilities Compared to Other Composite Membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: organosilica membrane (methyl silica sols - bis (triethoxysilyl) octane),

head.ce:title: Development of nanocomposite membranes based on sulfated β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde with magnetically recoverable magnetite-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles for water vapor dehumidification

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Hanbat National University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Hanbat National University, 125 Dongseodero, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 34158, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, we report sulfated β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde-coated (sb-CD/GA) TFN membranes with highly magnetically recoverable magnetite-carbon dot hybrid nanoparticles (CDs-Fe3O4) realized by enhancing the water vapor dehumidification. The Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs are successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method and incorporated into sulfated-β-cyclodextrin/glutaraldehyde (sb-CD/GA)-based thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes. Sb-CD is also very hydrophilic due to the presence of several reactive sites, specifically sulfonic acid groups, on its external surface. Therefore, the addition of hydrophilic sb-CD to the composite membranes facilitates the transport of water vapor. Moreover, one characteristic of Fe3O4–CDs particles is that they have a variety of hydrophilic groups which allow them to absorb more water vapor at a relatively rapid rate, with the addition of nanoparticles in the TFN membranes increasing the water vapor permeation due to the enhanced sorption sites and surface area provided by the nanoparticles. According to experimental results, the addition of Fe3O4–CDs to the sb-CD/GA -coated TFN membrane improves the water vapor permeance from 1376 M(0) to 2232 GPU M(0.3) wt% Fe3O4–CDs loading, improving the water vapor/N2 selectivity as well from 105 M(0) to 724 (M(0.3) at a relative humidity rate of 70%. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study of a sb-CD/GA-coated TFN membrane incorporated with highly functionalized Fe3O4–CDs to enhance water vapor dehumidification.

ce:section.ce:para: Water vapor removal from moist air is a time-consuming and energy-demanding process. Dehumidification is a critical component of air conditioning, particularly in Middle Eastern countries and other such locales where temperatures can reach 40–45 °C and relative humidity (RH) levels can reach 80–85% [1–4]. Cooling and dehumidification are needed to maintain comfortable temperatures (around 25 °C and 45% RH) while preventing the development of fungus and extending the lifetimes of buildings [5–7]. Dehumidification requires a considerable amount of energy, which means high power bills and a large carbon footprint [2,8]. For example, In the summer, air conditioning accounts for around 52% of total energy consumption in Saudi Arabia [8]. Moreover, the development of more energy-efficient processes that can replace those that consume more energy and replace the use of harmless materials as well are objectives that address green chemistry principles that should be applied to organic and petrochemical production processes in addition to widely used domestic operating systems. This type is associated with dehumidification, which has been linked to excessive energy saving or the use of potentially nontoxic and harmless materials.[9,10] This study proposed a low-energy dehydration process using harmless materials. Water vapor can inhibit targeted separation processes, and its occurrence in waterways can cause condensate lines to rust. For these reasons, desiccant or condensation-based methods have traditionally been used for dehumidification, but they have high running prices, are difficult to operate, are expensive, and use significant amounts of energy [2,6,7,11–13]. On the other hand, membrane-based dehumidification is increasingly seen as a long-term solution for dehydrating fumes and natural gas, recovering organic and other vapors, drying packaging materials, and controlling humidity levels in homes and buildings that use heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems [4,14,15]. Moreover, membrane-based technology does not require thermal regeneration or a phase change and thus consumes less energy. Furthermore, it is compact, energy-efficient, easy to scale, requires a low initial investment and is simple to use and maintain. Therefore, membrane-based technology can be an attractive alternative feasible for hybridization with existing technologies or for replacing existing technologies to remove water vapor.

Membrane-based separation techniques are attractive for water vapor dehumidification because water vapor molecules are smaller and more condensable than other light gases. In addition, the structure of the membranes is crucial to an effective membrane dehumidification operation. The coating can be made from a diversity of materials and has advantages over simple polymer membranes and inorganic membranes. Membranes with high water vapor permeance but no selectivity benefit from porous support materials due to the strengthened mechanical stability. The upper active skin layer is the determining factor in the overall permeability and selectivity of the membrane, and due to the structure and type of this layer, it plays an important role in a variety of applications [16–21]. A previously sulfonated polyether ketone [14], a mixture of cellulose acetate and polyethylene glycol [polydopamine [18], polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) [1], and poly (vinyl alcohol) [22] have been used as coatings on porous supports of polyethersulfone [18], polyacrylonitrile [1,23], and polysulfone [16,17] for the dehumidification of air. Bentonite clay [24] nano-fillers of graphene oxide-titanium oxides [25], titanosilicates [26], and dopamine [18] in the active layers were driven by interfacial polymerization to form a thin layer of a selective nanocomposite on porous fiber supports, acting as sorbents for vapor sorption. High-thermal-resistance polybenzimidazole [2], cellulose [27], cyclodextrin [28], and block copolymers such as poly(ether-block-amide) (PEBAX®) with graphene oxide nanosheets [29] and Nexar [30,31] have been shown to exhibit reasonable water vapor transport rates and high selectivity. However, the IP process uses organic solvents, including hexane, which are harmful to the environment. Additionally, this process requires large tanks for the immersion of the fibers, and these are difficult to drain. In order to create an environmentally friendly manufacturing process, many methods have been developed to reduce or eliminate the solvent, including vapor-phase interfacial polymerization (VPS) [32]. Aqueous phase separation (APS) is a newly proposed method that relies on changes in the pH or salt concentration to induce complexation of aqueous polyelectrolytes, therefore forming membranes. In the current study, we suggest an idea wherein hollow ﬁber membranes are coated via an aqueous-based method using effective nontoxic materials and recoverable Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. Herein, a sb-CD/GA-based nanocomposite membrane with capable of high dehumidification with the incorporation of green magnetically recoverable magnetite–carbon-dot hybrid nanoparticles (Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs) is demonstrated. The aim is to develop a safe and long-term system for the use of safer solvents, such as water, as well as nontoxic and recoverable nanoparticles. Furthermore, the cyclodextrins are torus-shaped molecules with a hydrophobic cavity and a hydrophilic exterior surface made up of cyclic 1,4 oligoglucopyranosides. β-CD is widely studied due to its low cost and due to the fact that its cavity is large enough to bind a wide range of aromatic and aliphatic molecules [33]. They can form inclusion complexes by incorporating numerous organic molecules into the central cavity [34]. Cyclodextrins have been widely deliberated in studies related to separation in this field due to their ability to form inclusion complexes [34–37]. Moreover, sb-CD/GA-based composite membranes provide tortuous pathways for water vapor and increased numbers of reactive sites (–SO3H). sb-CD is also very hydrophilic due to the presence of several reactive sites, specifically sulfonic acid groups, on its external surface. In addition, it exhibits greater water solubility by ten times compared to unmodified β-CD. Therefore, the addition of hydrophilic sb-CD to composite membranes facilitates the transport of water vapor, as the number of reactive sites is increased. Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs have been applied many application fields due to their unique characteristics, such as a low cost, environmental friendliness, easy removal, and good thermal stability. In addition, carbon dots (CDs), small nanocarbon particles, are useful for various applications in technology-related fields, such as optoelectronic devices, solar cells, detection and bioimaging [38–40]. Due to their high dispersion, alignment, and good water coverage, CDs are particularly suitable for separation applications [41]. Moreover, recoverable Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs have many hydrophilic functional groups, such as –NH2, –COOH, and –OH, that can be successfully synthesized by a hydrothermal method and incorporated into sb-CD/GA-based composite membranes for dehumidification. sb-CD/GA-based composite membranes are thus a more environmentally friendly substitute for traditional solvents such as hexane, and the magnetic nanoparticles extracted from reaction mixtures through magnetic separation represent significant criterion for biological and economic concerns [42–44]. For all of these reasons, the development of membranes using Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs may lead to effective applications to water vapor dehumidification.

Polysulfone (PSf) membranes (MWCO = 6000) were provided by Guiyang Shidai Huitong Film Technology Co. Ltd. (China). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (99%), iron (II) sulphate heptahydrate (98.5%), β-cyclodextrin, sulfated sodium salt (99%), HCl, and NaOH were obtained from Sigma–Aldrich. Glutaraldehyde (50 wt%in H2O) was obtained from Sigma–Aldrich and ethylenediamine was purchased from Junsei Chemicals. Chitosan (low molecular weight, 99%) was acquired from Sigma Aldrich and used as the carbon source for the synthesis of carbon dot nanoparticles. Purified water (DI) was acquired from a Milli-Q ultrapure water purification system. Potassium dihydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4, 95%) and ethanol were acquired from Duksan Chemical. All chemical used in this study were used as received.

In this method, FeCl3 (1.14 g) and FeSO4 (0.57 g) was dissolved in 150 ml of water. Subsequently, 10 ml of aqueous NaOH was added up to a pH of 11. The prepared solution was then poured into a 200 ml autoclave. After sealing the autoclave, it was heated to 200 °C for 6 h. The black-brown precipitate was separated by centrifugation (7000 rpm, 5 min) and then washed with water two times and with acetone for three minutes. The acquired solid particles was dispersed in ethanol and then removed magnetically. Subsequently, 0.5 gm of Fe3O4 was dispersed in 50 ml of H2O and an amount of 0.2 g of carbon dots synthesized from chitosan by a hydrothermal method, as shown in Fig. S2, was added. Later, 3 ml of ethylene diamine solution was added to the solution, which was then relocated into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave heated to 200 °C for 2 hr [41]. The precipitate was then washed with ethanol. Lastly, the brown precipitate was dried at 50 °C. Henceforth, it is denoted as the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs.

The sb-CD/ GA TFN membrane was fabricated using a solution coating method. The composite membrane was fabricated from a 1% (w/w) β-cyclodextrin solution as an aqueous solution and a 0.5% (w/w) glutaraldehyde solution. A crosslinked composite membrane was fabricated from a solution made by liquefying a mixing mass for 1 h at 60 °C until a homogenous solution was achieved and added HCl solution at 80 °C for 2 h to attain the cross-linking of sb-CD molecules [37]. The proposed crosslinking scheme is presented in ( Fig. 1). sb-CD/GA forms a crosslinked structure in the polysulfone matrix with a cavity on the upper side during the modification process, allowing it to form an inclusion complex [45,46]. Moreover, the different amount of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs added to the aqueous phase containing the sb-CD glutaraldehyde solution are presented in Table S1. The PSf membrane was immersed in the aforementioned solution for 15 min before draining for 2–3 min to remove any extra solution. The prepared membrane was then dried in an oven at 70 °C for 15 min. The condensation reaction takes place on the outer surface of the PSf membrane and following the development of a thin film of a sb-CD/GA/Fe3O4-CDs TFN membrane. The membrane for gas transport is governed by the solution-diffusion mechanism. In order for water vapor to pass through the membrane, water molecules are absorbed on the membrane surface and diffuse through cavities formed by the thermal fluctuation of the polymer chain. Hydrophilic particles incorporated into the membrane not only boost water adsorption on the membrane surface and but also favor the adsorption of water in competitive adsorption with nitrogen. This is in contrast to other Fe3O4-CDs, in which metals or metal clusters are coordinated by carbon dots (CDs) with various functional groups. Thus, the functional moiety acts as cavities, increasing the surface area. These cavities provide faster water transport channels and boost the diffusion of water. [25] Detailed structural characterization results will be discussed later in this paper.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of Fe3O4-CD hybrid NPs and TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) on a Thermo Scientific spectrometer was used to identify the surface chemistry of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs and TFN membranes. The distribution of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs and their size were examined using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) with a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer served to examine the changes in the crystalline characteristics of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. The Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs were elementally analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a MultiLab 2000 spectrometer. A vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) was used to test the magnetic properties of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs at room temperature. SEM images were taken at a voltage of 5 kV for a SEM analysis and at 20 kV for an EDX analysis. After exposing the membranes to liquid nitrogen for two minutes, a cross-section of the samples was created by cracking them. The water contact angle measured on a Phoenix 300 Plus device by SEO Co., Ltd. In order to determine the surface hydrophilicity of the membranes, the volume of the water droplets was measured and found to be 1.17 [uL]. The surface roughness of the membranes was analyzed in air using an Innova-LabRAM HR800 atomic force microscope.

An apparatus identified in our previous work was used to investigate membrane dehumidification, as shown in Fig. S1 [16]. At RH 70%, the permeance and selectivity of sb-CD/GA composite membranes with different Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs loadings from 0 to 0.5 w/w% were measured. In addition, the effect of the relative humidity on the membrane efficiency outcome was investigated for M(0.3). For all of the samples, the experimental conditions for dehumidification were 30 °C, 3 kgf cm-2 of pressure, and a feed flow rate of 1000 ml min−1.

Eq. (1) was used to calculate the water vapor flux:

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical structure of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The results of the XRD analysis of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs are shown in ( Fig. 2.) According to JCPDS card No. 85–1436, the planes of 111, 220, 311, 400, 422, 511, and 440 in the traditional XRD correspond with regular Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs [47,48]. This shows that using aqueous NaOH and longer hydrothermal periods to create well-crystallized Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs through hydrothermal techniques is advantageous. In a previous review, this change was observed due to the use of aqueous NaOH in the synthesis of Fe3O4 NPs [48]. It is thought that aqueous NaOH offers a reductive atmosphere that helps to reduce iron precursors and assist nucleation to realize successive growth. The comparatively small concentration and weak crystallinity of the carbon dot nanoparticles in the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs could explain why no typical reflection of carbon dots in 20–25° was observed [49,50].

In contrast, the particle morphology and size of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs shown in ( Fig.(3a-b)) are fairly uniform. HR-TEM was carried out to check the morphology. The Fe3O4 particles were in the range of 15–20 nm and the carbon dots were in the range of 1.3–7 nm, as shown in (Fig.3). To confirm the elemental content further, element mapping was conducted using EDX, as depicted in (Fig.(3c-h)). The element mapping outcome clearly indicates that the elements of C, O, N and Fe are present in the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. The element mapping results also show that carbon dots are well dispersed with the Fe3O4 particles.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chemical structure of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs
ce:section.ce:para: XPS was used to examine the surface properties of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. The XPS spectra indicate that the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs is composed of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and iron, as shown in Fig. 4a. The high-resolution spectra of C1s of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs (Fig.4b) can be deconvoluted at 284.4, 285.9, and 287.9 eV, allocated to carbon in the form of C–C, C–N, and C–O, respectively. Correspondingly, spectra of O 1 s were ﬁtted to three peaks at 529.8, 532.4, and 532.9 eV, allocated to oxygen in the form of carbonyl oxygen in CO and alkoxy oxygen in C−O (Fig. 4c). The high-resolution spectra (Fig. 4d) of N1s display two signals at 399.1 and 400.5, showing that nitrogen occurs typically in the form of pyridinic-like N and pyrrolic-like N [50,51]. XPS signals at 710.5 and 724.7 eV, which resemble to Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2, respectively, are the typical peaks for Fe3O4 NPs (Fig.4e). Therefore, the chemical bonds between the functional groups containing carboxylic acid, metal oxide and an amine group were confirmed.

The FT-IR spectra were also studied to assess the chemical functionality of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. In Fig. 5, the main groups at 3275 cm−1, 1620 cm−1, and 1428 cm−1 correspond to O–H, C–O, stretching vibration, and C–O bending vibration, respectively. The groups at 550 cm−1 and 443 cm−1 correspond to Fe–O bonds [47,50]. The binding of carbon dots to the surfaces of the Fe3O4 NPs is indicated by the intense band at 1620 cm−1 for Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. Carbon dot nanoparticles as a coordinating ligand could stabilize the Fe3O4 NPs to some extent, but they would have no effect on the magnetic characteristics and would hardly restrain the carbon dots on the surfaces of the Fe3O4-CD hybrid NPs [51–53].

VSM was used to test the magnetic properties of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs at room temperature. The magnetization of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs is shown in ( Fig. 6). The saturation magnetization of Fe3O4–CD was 50.6 emu/g. The large gap in the saturation magnetization in the Fe3O4–CD cases is mainly attributed to better crystallinity and magnetic properties [51]. In addition, the longer hydrothermal period improved the magnetism of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs while also increasing the crystallinity [47]. We could use this technique for the magnetic recoverability of the nanomaterial.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of the sb-CD/GA TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The FT-IR spectra of the PSf, M(0), M(0.1), M(0.3)and M(0.5) TFN membranes are revealed in Fig. 7. The group at 1152 cm−1 is allocated to the C–O–C group of polysulfone; those at 1245 cm−1 and 1586 cm−1 indicate the appearance of the sulfone group. In addition, the absorption band of the sulfone group is readily apparent, which proves the successful addition of the sb-CD during the development of an ether linkage as a result of crosslinking between the sb-CD with glutaraldehyde. The bands in the ranges of 1021–849 cm− 1, 2971–2948 cm−1, and 2878–2861 cm−1 stem from the C–H asymmetric and symmetric stretching of methyl groups related to the C atom in the PSf structure. The development of an ether linkage as a result of crosslinking between the sb-CD with glutaraldehyde is attributed to the band at 1749–1726 cm−1 in the spectra of the M(0), M(0.1), M(0.3)and M(0.5) TFN membranes. The absorbance in the range of 1149–1051 cm− 1 is a result of C–O–C and C–O stretching caused by sb-CD crosslinking with glutaraldehyde [37,54]. This stems from the C–O bond and confirms the presence of Fe3O4–CD nanoparticles in the TFN layer. The width of 3300 cm− 1 in all of the TFN membranes indicates the stretching of free (–OH), providing evidence of the free contact among the amino groups in the membrane structures. [37,54].

SEM surface images of membranes as well as cross-section images are shown in Fig. 8. As depicted in this figure, the membrane surface show a dense and defect-free layer, unlike the porous PSf support, and a different morphology was observed depending on the embedded Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. The M(0) membrane exhibits a nodular surface structure, which is a typical morphology for the sb-CD/GA membrane. [37] When Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs were added to the aqueous phase, a sphere-like surface was easily observed owing to the rapid diffusion of sb-CD attributed to Fe3O4–CD, which can contain water containing the sb-CD monomer. In the M(0) case, it showed a smooth surface. The pores in M(0.3) are fewer in number than those in PSf due to the existence of a crosslinked network in the PSf matrix as well as the presence of Fe3O4 hybrid NPs. These findings indicate that the incorporation of hydrophilic Fe3O4-CD hybrid NPs in the M(0.3) TFN membrane reduces the membrane porosity. The cross-section of the membranes shown in Fig. 8 specifies the variation in the micro-cavities. The cavities are wide in PSf compared to those the M(0.3) TFN membrane [37]. The incorporation of nanoparticles causes the thickness of the layer to increase, and this trend can be found in other studies. This result is likely stems from the fact that the hydrophilic nanoparticles absorbed a considerable amount of water containing sb-CD and more sb-CD participated to form the TFN layer. With an increase in the nanoparticle loading, The thickness of this layer is 254 nm, 338 nm, and 458 nm for M(0), M(0.3), and M(0.5), respectively.

To confirm that these Fe3O4–CD nanoparticles were present in the TFN layer, EDX tests of the PSf, M(0), M(0.1),M(0.3) and M(0.5) TFN membranes were conducted, where the Fe3O4–CD nanoparticles existing in the surface layer were assessed according to the loading concentration, as shown in Fig. S2. The Fe concentration gradually increased from rises from 0 to 0.68 at% when Fe content increases from 0.0 to 0.5 w/w% which clearly indicates that the incorporation of nanoparticle in the TFN layer.

The AFM images of the PSf and sb-CD/GA/ Fe3O4-CD hybrid NPs TFN membranes illustrated in ( Fig. 9) shows a typical hills-and-valleys morphology. The surface roughness of a membrane is a significant factor to consider when interpreting these types of results. Accordingly, an AFM analysis was carried out to monitor the change in the surface roughness after the thin layer was formed, as illustrated in Fig. 6. The AFM data revealed a substantial increase in the roughness compared to that of the PSf substrate (Rq = 18.3 nm, Ra = 13.3 nm and Rmax = 141 nm) when the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NP concentration was increased from 0 to 0.5 wt%. Furthermore, M(0) showed lower roughness compared to the M(0.05) membranes (Rq = 29.9 nm, Ra = 22.5 nm and Rmax = 271 nm). Also, the M(0.1) membrane exhibited surface roughness (Rq = 34.6 nm, Ra = 27 nm, Rmax = 274 nm), and among the M(0–0.3) membranes that exhibited high roughness, M(0.3) showed the highest roughness (Rq = 50.2 nm, Ra = 39 nm and Rmax = 359 nm). M(0.5) meanwhile showed lower roughness (Rq = 47.8 nm, Ra = 37 nm and Rmax = 344 nm) due to the high number of particles agglomerated in the composite membrane. The increase in the roughness could be ascribed to the incorporation of nanoparticles, suggesting that the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs perform an important role in increasing the TFN membrane surface roughness parameter. The increase in the surface area of the sb-CD/GA composite membrane membranes caused by the change in the mean surface roughness of the thin layer after the incorporation of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs into the polymer matrix improved the water vapor permeation of the coated membranes. The theory of solution-diffusion is renowned in gas separation membranes [1,55]. The hydrophilicity of a membrane is an important factor determining its performance [20]. The hydrophilicity of a TFN membrane is determined by the types of coating materials, their capacity to interact with water molecules, and the surface roughness.

The water contact angle is commonly used to calculate the degree of membrane hydrophilicity, and these two variables are inversely proportional. In Fig. 10 shows the contact angles of the PSf and coated TFN membranes. The hydrophilicity of the membrane was found to increase as the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NP content in the aqueous solution increased. The M(0.3) specimen had the higher hydrophilicity at 18° compared to the PSf membrane. This value shows that the TFN membranes are highly hydrophilic, which is advantageous for water vapor permeation. This is related to the high loading of nanoparticles, which are bounded by NH2, COOH groups, and OH groups, enabling the membrane surface to interact efficiently with water molecules via hydrogen bonding and providing more active sites for permeation [56]. From the contact angle results, it was confirmed that the Fe3O4–CD-incorporated TFN membranes had superior hydrophilic properties. In addition to the increased surface roughness of the Fe3O4–CD membranes, this increase in the hydrophilicity led to an improvement in the water vapor permeance. Additionally, adding Fe3O4–CD particles to the thin film increased the number of adsorption sites to draw water vapor molecules [39]. During the process of flue gas dehydration, it is significant to increase the selectivity of membranes to manage the relative humidity so as to prevent white fumes. The M(0.3) membranes can effectively reduce white fumes coming out from factories because they have excellent water vapor permeance and selectivity. These results are in good agreement with the EDX, AFM, and FT-IR results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance of sb-CD/GA nanocomposite membranes for dehumidification
ce:section.ce:para: The dehumidification test was carried out using the system shown in Fig. S1 to investigate the significance of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs with regard to improving the membrane efficiency [56]. Fig. 10 shows the effect of the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NP loading level on the permeance and selectivity outcomes. The solution-diffusion mechanism, which involves three key phases, feasibly describes the transport of water vapor and N2 through a dense membrane [55]. The gases are initially adsorbed at the membrane's feed side and then diffuse through the membranes, eventually desorbing on the permeate side. From the results illustrated in ( Fig. 11), it was found that increasing the Fe3O4–CD hybrid NP loading level led to increased water vapor permeance/N2 selectivity up to the specific loading rate of 0.3% (w/w). The incorporation of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs into the selective TFN layer considerably improves the water permeance along with the selectivity under all conditions. The entire TFN membrane exhibit increased water permeance with selectivity as the particle concentration increases to 0.3 w/w%, implying that the incorporated nanoparticles make the TFN surface more hydrophilic and provide additional water transport channels. However, given the valley point at 0.5 w/w%, increasing the particle concentration further reduces both the permeance and selectivity. This can be ascribed to the aggregation of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs, which inevitably induces non-selective membrane defects on the TFN surface. Among the coated membranes, the M(0.3) membrane exhibited the best water vapor permeance of 2232 GPU and selectivity of 724, stemming from the fact that M(0.3) exhibited higher hydrophilicity, surface roughness parameters, and relative surface area. A further increment in the nanoparticle loading rate above 0.3% (w/w) results in decreased permeance and selectivity of the composite membranes. However, the further addition of Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs above 0.3%(w/w) led to a decrement in the selectivity and water permeance. Here, water transport is facilitated by the special water trails provided by sb-CD molecules with Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs and hence the enhancement of the membrane performance.

A constant-pressure apparatus was used to investigate the effect of the relative humidity (RH) on the efficiency of the M(0.3) membrane, which showed the best water vapor permeance and selectivity at 30 °C, as shown in Fig. 12. The water vapor permeance increased from 1638 to 2335 GPU and the selectivity improved from 297 to 753 when the RH was increased from 30% to 80%. The solubility of water vapor within membrane chains is said to be a property of its partial pressure activity [28,55]. In addition, according to the dual-mode sorption model, increasing the feed humidity causes more water to adsorb onto the membrane surface [57].

The effect of the feed gas flow rate within the range of 1–6 L/min on the membrane performance was investigated for M(0.3) at a feed gas pressure, temperature, and relative humidity of 3 bar, 30 °C, and RH= 70%, respectively. The permeance and water flux increased until they reached a certain point and then stabilized. The first condition of increased water flux and permeance can be ascribed to the higher operative water capturing rate and to the decrease in the concentration polarization due to increased turbulence at a higher gas velocity [58,59]. Steady flux of M(0.3) was attained at a feed gas flow rate of 6 L/min at 0.44 kg/m2/hr, as shown in Fig. S3.

A sequential modification process was used to create sb-CD/GA TFN membranes, which included adding Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs to the coating solution and preparing a selective layer on the surface of PSf. All processes in this study were carried out under identical conditions, and the effects of functionalized Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs on the efficiency of the sb-CD/GA TFN membrane were observed visually. Table 1 summarizes the comparative results.

To summarize, the Fe3O4–CD-incorporated TFN membranes played an important role in improving water vapor dehumidification. The performance of these membranes was affected differently by changes in the Fe3O4–CD concentration, the second type of monomer (sb-CD/ GA), and the operating conditions. The optimal performance achieved in this study was compared with other outcomes in the literature, as shown in Table 1. This table illustrates that the Fe3O4–CD-coated membranes showed excellent performance compared to most data from previous research.

Polysulfone hollow ﬁber membranes were coated via an aqueous-based method using effective nontoxic materials and recoverable Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs. Herein, an sb-CD/GA-based nanocomposite membrane with high dehumidification proficiency was proven with the incorporation of magnetically recoverable magnetite–carbon-dot hybrid nanoparticles (Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs) as a favorable candidate nanomaterial for membrane-based dehumidification. The physicochemical and structural qualities of Fe3O4–CDs hybrid NPs and their composite membranes were investigated using a variety of techniques. From HR-TEM mapping, it was confirmed that the Fe3O4 nanoparticles were well encapsulated by carbon dot nanoparticles. The use of safer solvents, preferably water, as well as nontoxic and recoverable nanoparticles has been a key strategy for achieving a clean and sustainable process. Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs with many hydrophilic functional groups, such as -COOH and –OH, were well synthesized by a hydrothermal method and incorporated into the sb-CD/GA-based TFN membrane for water vapor dehumidiﬁcation. The experimental results indicate that the TFN membrane incorporating the 0.3%(w/w) Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs showed good performance, as evaluated based on a water vapor permeance outcome of 2232 GPU, flux of 0.44 kg/m2·hr, and selectivity of 724 at RH 70%. From these outcomes, we propose that the sb-CD/GA-based nanocomposite membrane with recoverable Fe3O4–CD hybrid NPs is a favorable candidate for dehumidification and that this method may offer a chance to project a clean and sustainable method to prepare TFN membranes with improved performance.

Yogita M. Shirke: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ali M. Abou-Elanwar: Data curation. Soon Jin Kwon: Visualization. Won-Kil Choi: Visualization. Seong Uk Hong: Supervision. Hyung Keun Lee: Supervision. Jae-Deok Jeon: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jece.2021.107042.
